# Transcrição: 07-Financier Due Diligence

- **Fonte:** 07-Financier Due Diligence
- **Duração:** 13:04
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:03]

We're through financial due diligence, commercial due diligence, and legal due diligence. Now we're moving into the financier due diligence part of this module. And what I mean by financier due diligence is the due diligence that your potential lender is going to do on the company you're interested in buying. This could also involve equity investors. But to keep things simple for the purpose of this lesson, we're going to focus primarily on lenders and the due diligence they'll do. Namely fact checking the financial background of the target company borrowing the money for the leverage buyout. Now the fact checking that's involved here is much more complex in regards to a share sale than an asset sale. And the reason for this is in a share sale, you're not only responsible for your current liabilities, you're also somewhat responsible for all past liabilities. You want to make sure that your lender is looking at any potential past liabilities. That's why they're doing the due diligence and it's a lot more extensive in a share sale. For example, I know someone who is consulting on a deal where a large technology company was buying the assets of a small technology company. The smaller company accepted an offer for about $27 million and was promptly handed a questionnaire with over 500 questions, many of them in multiple parts. Now a financier who's reviewing the finances for the smaller company, like the kind we're targeting with leverage buyouts, they're going to have a much shorter list of questions than that. But I bring up this example just to let you know that what we're going to cover in this lesson is by no means exhaustive. The financiers do diligence in questions that they'll be asking you far beyond what we're going to be talking about here. And to be honest, they always vary according to the specific situation or business you're going to buy. But this lesson is going to show you some of the types of things that you're going to be looking at, the level of detail that they're going to go into and the kinds of questions they will ask. Please keep in mind that they're going to need a lot of information, both from you and the seller and potentially third parties. So let's get started. We're going to begin with the financial reporting. First of all, your lender will want to see the financial statements going back at least three years. These have to be the financial statements signed by management and including all the notes prepared by the accountants. They'll also want to see them up to date, interim financial statements. So if the company's fiscal year begins January 1st and you're doing a deal in July, your financier is going to want to see everything through June. When it comes to lending for the purchase of the business, which is typically part of the process, they'll want to look at the numbers as close to the day of closing as possible. But for their purposes at this point, they'll request the financial statements and the interim statements to be the latest completed month. And they'll want to see current detailed statements generated from the accounting system. They do not want to see a reproduced spreadsheet prepared by the seller. Your financier will also want to see any financial projections that were created in the past.

## [03:03 - 06:03]

This is a projection from the bottom up, month by month, product by product, and it's not top down, which is taking the number from last year and just applying a factor scale. Bottom up is more detailed, and that's what they're going to want to see. And if there isn't one, you'll need to have your accountant create it. They're also going to ask for copies of the accounting or controllers manual and the related policies that were followed to prepare those financial statements. And of course, there are numerous other items your lender will want to see as part of their normal due diligence, such as three months of bank statements. And remember, they need to see the original documents, including all the secondary accounts. So make sure those are compiled, as well as cash reconciliation over the last six months to a year, once again, including all the secondary accounts. It always helps to anticipate what they're going to want to see and try to have as much of it put together as possible. Due diligence is an involved and lengthy process, so it really just helps to move things along. Your lender will also need to know that the check authorization and signing authority procedures that the target company follows, as well as any other changes made over the last five years. And it will help here by preparing a list of the names and titles of everyone who's had signing authority during that time. They'll also want to know what your current cash control protocol and procedures are right now. Or if they don't have one, they'll ask you to put one in place. Just go ahead and put one in place when asked. Don't try to set one up and pretend it's been around for a while. They'll be able to tell. What's important here is the lenders are looking for assurance that the cash is under lockdown and that there are strong cash control mechanics in place for the business. This is very important to the lender requirement. If the seller doesn't currently have this in place, the lender will want to see that you have the discipline to do that. Sales reporting is also important. Your lender is going to ask for a sample purchase order invoice for any three to five completed deals. You want to make sure that they're most recent deals. And if possible, try to make sure you use a customer that has good name recognition. It never hurts to let your great clients brag about your company or potential company when working with lenders. As part of this step, the lender will also want to see the terms and conditions of standard customer contract or invoice, or at the very least, a customer agreement. If there have been any recent changes, be sure to include the provisions there. The lender wants to see what's driving engagement with customers now. They'll also ask for more information like 10 or 20 largest customers, as well as their longevity and volume. And if possible, your financier might want to see detailed sales forecasts going out two years or so, as well as the financial projections for the entire company. With the sales forecast here, the lender will want to see things broken down into a little bit more detail. They'll want to see the major product categories and how they project out for a two-year period. So they're also going to ask quite a few questions around taxes and tax reporting. What they're looking for is that the seller has done this timely and accurately, and that you're familiar with the need to do this. In addition, if you're in a jurisdiction that has VAT, which is value-added tax, you're going to make sure that that's filed and recorded monthly and paid appropriately.

## [06:03 - 09:03]

The lender will also want to see the last three years of the seller's corporate taxes and that they've been paid and their current and up-to-date. And if you're in the U.S., this means the IRS. You'll want to supply the originals here, or at least copies of the originals, as well as any correspondence the company had with tax authorities going back five years. This includes audits, reassessments, requests for information. All of this is going to be part of your due diligence, not only on a federal level, but on a state level. And trust me, these need to be thorough because there will be indemnities involved. The seller will be indemnifying you against any past obligation that they've not disclosed or that they have penalties associated with. And your lender will want this indemnification as well. So if something comes up they didn't disclose, they're definitely going to be on the hook for it. Your lender is going to want to see up-to-date information on the assets and receivables. So not a summary, but a detailed account generated from the accounting system that includes the age of the receivables or a debtor schedule. You need all this information in case there's carve-outs, so the lender can carve out the receivables that don't qualify for financing, whether it's age or another reason. They'll also want to see a list of invoices that have been factored, as well as any invoices that are subject to a bond because they'll want those carved out as well. This is especially common in construction-type companies, so it might not apply to your situation, but it could apply to different situations. Your lender also needs to see a list of bad debts. That is, any invoices over the past few years have not been paid. It may take a little time to do this, but it's a good idea to compile an item items list, if possible, especially to protect your future interest in the business. It's likely going to come into play later when they ask about fixed assets, and when that time comes they'll want to see the asset register with the full-make model, year of manufacture, etc., including the purchase price and the net book value for the asset, because they're going to go through all the assets, asset by asset. If you're buying the business, you should pay particular attention to putting this together before you talk to your lender so they don't think you don't see what's going on in the business. They'll also want to see a list broken out of the assets already financed with existing debt, including the information for the specific loan secured by each asset. Then they'll go through and match any debt to the current balance sheet against the assets that secures the debt. As you can see, it's a bit of a process. That's why they ask for so much information. They want to make sure all this stuff is itemized out and that you're tracking and they're tracking to it to make things easier at closing. Your financier will also want to see the capital expenditure plan for the next three years. We've talked about capital expenditures in some of our previous lessons, so I won't dwell on it too much here. Sometimes companies can be late on taking on capital expenditures leading up to an acquisition. If they're trying to maximize cash flow for a year or two for an acquisition, they may put off capital expenditures that the business needs in order to succeed so that the new buyer needs to spend this money. So the lender will want to know that.

## [09:03 - 12:05]

That's also why they'll ask for inventory. So if your target company has a lot of inventory, expect a lot of questions about it. First you'll want to compile a detailed list of all the SKUs for inventory, including the most recent counts and costs. Your lender will also need to see what kind of inventory control system you're using to get that information because you're not going to get inventory financing without a fairly tight inventory control system. They'll ask questions like if you use FIFO or LIFO or if there have been any changes to the valuation methodology. And if that's the case, they'll also want the date the change was made because the changes in methodology can cause changes in the company's reported income. So that's very important to tie these out. You'll also need to provide company inventory data, including the inventory turns for the entire company as well as each individual product. The financier is going to need to know about any debt instruments, loans, debt facilities, or credit lines that are currently used to finance inventory. So you'll want to be able to answer those questions and provide the relevant information. You should also have a list of the seller's 20 largest suppliers, including their longevity and order volume, as well as the date of their most recent purchase if possible. It might be more than less than 20, but it's usually good to know exactly what that number is and how much money is traded between those suppliers. They'll also need to see detailed up-to-date creditor and payable schedules. Remember, most importantly, they want to make sure everything is accounted for and documented properly. If the deal includes land or property, there will be an additional amount of due diligence regarding that. If the lender is going to finance the property, they'll need to identify the party or parties that currently own the property so it can be transferred, because it's usually not the company that owns the property but individuals. That means you also need to identify any liens that are currently against the property, as well as provide all the surveys the seller is currently in possession of and the zoning of the property. You also need to create a list of all mortgages for the property, past, present, etc. For any present mortgages, include the remaining balance currently held against property, and keep in mind, sometimes there's more than one. Another thing that they'll be asking for is current appraisal documents, because the lender is going to want to see the documentation that supports the value of the property. As we wrap up this section, I want to cover just a few things you might be asked to provide. If there's a loan that will remain in place after the acquisition, they'll want to know the commercial loan schedule and financing instrument, as well as the covenants of that loan. Your lender will also want to see a list of all prepaid expenses. If something happens to be zero, but is still a prepaid account, just include it on the balance sheet as zero. This is also where you'll provide the letter of intent signed by both parties. So you should have that with you. Now is the time to start planning your company structure as well, because they're going to ask you about it. For example, who will be your directors? Who will be the shareholders? What percentage of ownership, etc., etc. They might even want to see some material on the company, such as a brochure or PDF.

## [12:05 - 13:04]

Now I warned you at the beginning your lender is going to likely ask a lot of questions. And we've probably gotten to 40 without even really digging into it. Just remember, your lender will ask you a lot of questions and they'll need to see as much documentation and facts as possible. They're not just asking the questions for the fun of it. They need to do a lot of detail and sometimes the buyer simply isn't prepared for the sheer amount of information they'll need to provide. And that does slow things down a bit. That's why we spent so much time in this lesson covering everything that you'll most likely need to provide to help move things along, as well as to ensure that you can have the confidence in your deal. Sometimes what can happen is a buyer will have a balance sheet from one period of time and a financial statement from another. And as they start pulling together more and more information, they take to the lender and they start to realize things are looking worse than they thought. So now that you know what the lender is going to ask for, you're better prepared to ask for the detailed questions about the target company you're interested in, especially at the beginning and in the letter of intent. That's all for now and I'll see you at the next lesson.
